description: A structured path through HSK 1–6, pairing characters with pinyin, grammar patterns, and practical vocabulary.

levels.title: HSK 1
levels.description: The first 150 core characters and the simplest sentence patterns.

lessons.title: Greetings & Introductions
lessons.summary: 你好 and the essentials of introducing yourself.
content: 你好 (nǐ hǎo) is the most common greeting in Mandarin, literally meaning 'you good'. It works in almost any situation, formal or casual.
To introduce your name, use 我叫... (wǒ jiào...), literally 'I am called...'. The question form 你叫什么名字？(nǐ jiào shénme míngzì?) asks 'what is your name?'.

lessons.title: Numbers & Counting
lessons.summary: 0–10 and the basic measure word 个.
content: Chinese numbers 1–10 (一 to 十) are the foundation for dates, prices, ages, and phone numbers — they appear in almost every conversation.
个 (gè) is the most common measure word, used before general nouns when counting: 三个人 (sān gè rén) means 'three people'.

levels.title: HSK 2
levels.description: Roughly 300 characters, covering daily routines and simple opinions.

lessons.title: Daily Routine
lessons.summary: Talking about your day from 起床 to 睡觉.
content: 起床 (qǐ chuáng) means 'to get up' and 睡觉 (shuì jiào) means 'to sleep' — together they bracket the daily routine vocabulary you'll use constantly at this level.
Time words like 早上 (zǎoshang, morning), 中午 (zhōngwǔ, noon), and 晚上 (wǎnshang, evening) usually appear at the start of a sentence, before the subject.

lessons.title: Simple Comparisons with 比
lessons.summary: 'A 比 B + adjective' — the core comparison pattern.
content: 比 (bǐ) means 'compared to'. The pattern A 比 B + adjective builds comparisons: 今天比昨天热 (jīntiān bǐ zuótiān rè) means 'today is hotter than yesterday'.
To say something is not more than another, use 没有 instead of 比: 今天没有昨天热 means 'today is not as hot as yesterday'.

levels.title: HSK 3
levels.description: Around 600 characters — enough for basic independent daily communication.

lessons.title: The 了 Particle
lessons.summary: Marking completed actions and changed states.
content: 了 (le) placed after a verb usually marks that an action is completed: 我吃了 (wǒ chī le) means 'I have eaten'.
了 placed at the end of a sentence can mark a change of state instead: 天黑了 (tiān hēi le) means 'it has gotten dark' — the sky wasn't dark before, and now it is.

vocabulary.translation: completed action / change of state particle

lessons.title: Making Plans
lessons.summary: Vocabulary for suggesting, agreeing, and scheduling.
content: 打算 (dǎsuàn) means 'to plan to', used before a verb: 我打算去北京 means 'I plan to go to Beijing'.
The suggestion pattern 我们...吧 (wǒmen...ba) softens a proposal into a friendly suggestion: 我们走吧 means 'let's go'.

levels.title: HSK 4
levels.description: Around 1200 characters, opening the door to fluent conversation on varied topics.

lessons.title: The 把 Construction
lessons.summary: Fronting the object to emphasize what happens to it.
content: The 把 (bǎ) construction moves the object before the verb to emphasize its disposal or result: 我把门关了 (wǒ bǎ mén guān le) means 'I closed the door', emphasizing what happened to the door specifically.
把 is only used when the verb has a clear result or the object is specific and known — it cannot be used with simple, non-disposal actions.

lessons.title: Expressing Opinions
lessons.summary: 认为, 觉得, and other ways to say what you think.
content: 觉得 (juéde) is used for personal feelings or impressions: 我觉得这个电影很好看 means 'I think this movie is very good'.
认为 (rènwéi) is more formal and analytical, often used for stated opinions or arguments rather than gut feelings.

levels.title: HSK 5
levels.description: Around 2500 characters — reading newspapers and watching films without subtitles.

lessons.title: Formal Written Connectors
lessons.summary: 尽管...但是..., 由于..., and other essay-level connectors.
content: 尽管...但是... (jǐnguǎn...dànshì...) means 'although...but...' and is common in formal writing and speeches to introduce a concession.
由于 (yóuyú) means 'due to' and typically opens a sentence to explain a cause, often followed by a result introduced with 因此 (yīncǐ, therefore).

lessons.title: Reading News Summaries
lessons.summary: Common structures in condensed news Chinese.
content: News writing favors compact noun phrases over full clauses: '经济增长放缓' (economic growth slowing) packs a whole idea into four characters.
Numbers and percentages are extremely common in news texts — practicing reading 百分之 (percent) and large numbers fluently pays off quickly at this level.

levels.title: HSK 6
levels.description: 5000+ characters — the top level, approaching native reading fluency.

lessons.title: Literary & Idiomatic Chengyu
lessons.summary: Four-character idioms that carry entire stories in a few strokes.
content: 画蛇添足 (huà shé tiān zú), literally 'draw a snake and add feet', describes ruining something by adding unnecessary detail — from a fable where a man lost a drinking contest by needlessly adding feet to his drawing.
Chengyu are dense with cultural reference; understanding the story behind each one makes it far easier to remember and to use correctly in writing.

vocabulary.translation: to ruin something by overdoing it

lessons.title: Nuanced Register: Formal vs. Colloquial
lessons.summary: Choosing between 你 and 您, and other register shifts.
content: At HSK6, the challenge shifts from vocabulary size to precision of register. 您 (nín) versus 你 (nǐ), or 询问 (xúnwèn, formal 'to inquire') versus 问 (wèn, plain 'to ask') — the meaning is nearly identical, but the tone is not.
Reading a wide range of genres — legal notices, literary fiction, spoken interviews — builds an intuitive feel for which register fits which context.
